words.definition: someone you know slightly but not well
example_sentences: He's just an acquaintance, not a close friend.
She has many acquaintances through work.
I ran into an old acquaintance at the supermarket.
words.pronunciation_tip: uh-KWAYN-tunss — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I know him a little' — say 'He's an acquaintance'.

words.definition: a strong emotional connection between people
example_sentences: There's a very strong bond between the two sisters.
They bonded over their shared love of music.
The experience created a lasting bond between them.
words.pronunciation_tip: BOND — short 'o'.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'connection' always — 'bond' is stronger and more emotional.

words.definition: reliable, honest, and can be depended on
example_sentences: She is completely trustworthy — I'd tell her anything.
He's looking for a trustworthy business partner.
A trustworthy friend is genuinely hard to find.
words.pronunciation_tip: TRUST-wur-thee — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I can trust him' as an adjective — say 'he is trustworthy'.

words.definition: the ability to understand and share another person's feelings
example_sentences: She showed great empathy for her colleague's difficult situation.
Empathy is essential in nursing and teaching.
He genuinely lacks empathy — he never understands others.
words.pronunciation_tip: EM-puh-thee — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse empathy (feeling with someone) with sympathy (feeling sorry for them).

words.definition: giving help, encouragement, or emotional support
example_sentences: Her family was incredibly supportive during her illness.
He's a supportive manager who really listens to his team.
Having a supportive partner makes an enormous difference.
words.pronunciation_tip: suh-PAWR-tiv — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'He helps me a lot' as an adjective — say 'He is very supportive'.

words.definition: a serious disagreement or argument between people
example_sentences: There was a significant conflict between the two colleagues.
She tries to avoid conflict wherever possible.
The conflict was eventually resolved through honest discussion.
words.pronunciation_tip: KON-flikt — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'they have a fight' for every disagreement — 'conflict' is more neutral.

words.definition: to become friendly again after a disagreement
example_sentences: They had a terrible argument but eventually reconciled.
It took many months to reconcile after the dispute.
She reconciled with her brother after years of silence.
words.pronunciation_tip: REK-un-syl — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'they became friends again' — say 'they reconciled'.

words.definition: always supporting and being faithful to someone
example_sentences: She has been completely loyal to the company for twenty years.
A loyal friend stands by you in the most difficult times.
He is fiercely loyal to his family above everything else.
words.pronunciation_tip: LOY-ul — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'She always supports him' as an adjective — say 'She is loyal to him'.

words.definition: a person you work with in the same organisation
example_sentences: She gets along very well with all her colleagues.
I'm meeting a former colleague for lunch today.
My colleagues were extremely supportive during my illness.
words.pronunciation_tip: KOL-eeg — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'my work friends' in professional contexts — say 'my colleagues'.

words.definition: a brother or sister
example_sentences: She has two siblings — an older brother and a younger sister.
Sibling relationships can be wonderfully complex.
Do you have any siblings?
words.pronunciation_tip: SIB-ling — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'brother/sister' when you want to refer to both — 'sibling' covers both.

words.definition: to respect and feel great appreciation for someone
example_sentences: I really admire her dedication and incredibly hard work.
He is widely admired throughout the company for his honesty.
She has always admired her mother's strength and patience.
words.pronunciation_tip: ad-MYR — stress on second syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I like him very much' for deep respect — say 'I admire him'.

words.definition: an experienced person who gives guidance and support
example_sentences: She had a wonderful mentor when she first started her career.
He mentored several young doctors during his career.
Having a good mentor can completely transform your career trajectory.
words.pronunciation_tip: MEN-tawr — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'teacher' for a personal career guide — say 'mentor'.

words.definition: feeling unhappy because someone else has something you want
example_sentences: She was jealous of her friend's rapid career success.
Don't be jealous — focus on your own achievements instead.
He felt jealous when he saw how happy they seemed together.
words.pronunciation_tip: JEL-us — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse jealous (wanting what others have) with envious (similar, slightly weaker).

words.definition: truly sincere, honest, and not pretending
example_sentences: She's a completely genuine person — what you see is what you get.
His concern for others always seems completely genuine.
I appreciated her genuine interest in my personal problem.
words.pronunciation_tip: JEN-yoo-in — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'real friend' — say 'genuine friend' for someone truly sincere.

words.definition: to recognise and be grateful for the value of something
example_sentences: I really appreciate everything you've done for me.
She doesn't fully appreciate how incredibly lucky she is.
He greatly appreciates honesty and directness in others.
words.pronunciation_tip: uh-PREE-shee-ayt — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I am thankful for' always — 'appreciate' is broader and more natural.
words.collocations: ['really appreciate', 'appreciate your help', 'appreciate the value of']

words.definition: determined not to change your opinion or behaviour
example_sentences: She's incredibly stubborn — she absolutely never admits she's wrong.
He stubbornly refused to apologise even though he knew he was wrong.
Being stubborn can be both a strength and a significant weakness.
words.pronunciation_tip: STUB-urn — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she is too strong in her opinion' — say 'she is stubborn'.

words.definition: an angry disagreement between two or more people
example_sentences: They had a big argument about money and expenses.
The argument lasted for several hours without resolution.
She tries hard to avoid arguments in the workplace.
words.pronunciation_tip: AR-gyoo-ment — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'they had a fight' — 'fight' implies physical conflict. Use 'argument' for verbal.

words.definition: willingness to accept behaviour or opinions different from your own
example_sentences: She shows remarkable tolerance for different cultures and customs.
The job requires a very high tolerance for stress and pressure.
Tolerance and respect for others are core human values.
words.pronunciation_tip: TOL-er-unss — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she accepts differences' as a noun — say 'she shows great tolerance'.

words.definition: to involve yourself in a situation that does not concern you
example_sentences: Stop interfering in my personal relationship!
His parents interfere far too much in his adult life.
I don't want to interfere but I'm genuinely worried about her wellbeing.
words.pronunciation_tip: in-ter-FEER — three syllables, stress on last.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'don't enter in my problems' — say 'don't interfere in my problems'.

words.definition: causing or feeling social embarrassment or difficulty
example_sentences: There was a horribly awkward silence when he walked in.
It was really awkward running into my ex at the party.
She felt deeply awkward being the only person who knew nobody.
words.pronunciation_tip: AWK-werd — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I felt strange and uncomfortable' — say 'I felt awkward'.
